findings.description: slugify truncates to max_length dead last, so NFKD normalization (L17), the ascii encode/decode round-trip (L18), three chained full-string allocations (L19), and two regex passes (L20-21) all run over the entire untruncated input — a 10MB string does megabytes of work and ~6 full copies just to keep 80 chars; truncate the raw input early since the output is capped regardless.

levenshtein is O(len(a)*len(b)) with no input-length guard on a public web-service entry point, so two ~100KB strings = ~10^10 operations and a hung worker; the algorithm itself is the correct/optimal two-row DP, but it needs a max-length cap (the deliberate-pathological-input DoS framing is deferred to critic-security).

The r"-{2,}" pattern is compiled inline on every slugify call while the module's other two patterns are precompiled module-level constants; Python's regex cache avoids a true recompile but you pay a cache lookup per call and it is inconsistent — promote it to a module-level _DASH_RUN_RE.
indent_block calls line.strip() purely as an emptiness test, allocating a throwaway stripped copy of every line; 'not line or line.isspace()' tests the same condition without the per-line allocation.
